If you're a real estate agent and you're tired of your prospects negotiating your commissions, you want to say this instead. Come over here. I'm going to show you what to do. So the first thing you want to do is you want to deframe them. Actually, called a D-frame right here. Okay. So write down D-frame because I've got to get them out of the belief system that, you know, they want to use a real estate agent that lowers the fee. Because that would obviously imply if a real estate agent was lowering their fees, they don't have much business because they're not that good at selling properties at the market value and on time. To take them from price or cost-based thinking to results-based thinking. What's in result of selling the home?
Them making more money, right? We're not even focused on the fee at that point. To do this. I'm going to act like I'm a little bit confused. Like, Oh yeah, we don't, we don't really have clients that would want me to like reduce the fee. I mean, is the, is the cheapest fee. important thing to you. We're actually selling the property in the next 90 days around that $1. 9 million market value price. See what I did there. That is part of the reframe. Reframing is the process of taking them from their belief system. Their belief system is, I want to use a real estate agent. I'm going to give them the listing that has the cheapest fee. In price or cost-based thinking.
I want to take them out of that belief system, that frame, and reframe them into the end result of me selling the property. On time and for the market value. Does that make sense? See how I take them out of that belief system into a new belief system, all right? So the first thing I'm going to do, I'm going to do this again today. I want you to pay attention to what I'm doing here. Oh, yeah, we don't really have clients that would want me to reduce the fee. I mean, is the cheapest fee the most important thing to you or actually like getting the property sold on time? Around the $1. 9 million market value price. Now, notice what I said here. Why did I say, why would I use the cheapest fee?
Okay. What does 'cheapest' sound like? Do you like 'cheapest'? It sounds kind of bad. Now, if I said, 'I mean, is the cheapest fee the most important thing to you or actually like getting the property on time?' See, that's not going to do anything. But the way my tonality emphasizes 'cheapest' as a bad thing. Watch what I'm doing here. Look at my hands. So is the cheapest fee the most important thing to you or actually selling the product? Property in the next 90 days around the $1. 9 million market value price. It's hard for them to say, 'No, the cheapest fee is most important over selling it on time for the market. Market value price.' Notice I repeat back the price they said they wanted the home to sell for.
Okay. I'm always going to do that. I'm not just going to repeat something like, 'or do you want the home sold on? time.' That's not going to get them into that results-based thinking. The number that they said is what I want them to think about. Are you with me on that? Okay. Now reframing is the processor. So I'm starting to reframe right here. I'm reframing them into a new belief system that we're lucky to have a real estate agent who charges the full fee because that must mean they're way better than anybody else that offering discounts. Right? Because an expert, an authority, that everybody's going to to sell the property to, that sells the most homes would never have to reduce their fee, right? They're too good, right?

So identity framing comes from people like Milton Erickson, you know, famous psych, you know, a family therapist back. What was a hundred years ago. Sigmund Freud, if you've learned a little bit about Sigmund Freud in a university, like your psychology classes, this is the same. It's identity framing.
